paper_title: SEAL: Synergistic Co-Evolution of Agents and Learning Environments

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Large Language Model (LLM) agents are increasingly improved through interaction, yet most self-evolution methods adapt either the policy or the learning environment in isolation. We identify this structural gap as \emph{Agent-Environment Misalignment}: the agent's capability frontier changes during training, while the environment that provides supervision remains static or only weakly coupled to the agent's revealed failures. We propose SEAL, a closed-loop co-evolution framework for interactive tool-use agents. SEAL collects on-policy trajectories under executable verification, diagnoses failed rollouts into turn-level failure labels, and uses these diagnoses as a shared signal for both environment-side adaptation and model-side policy optimization. The environment evolves its training-time learning interface by exposing clearer tool affordance cues, constraint information, and recovery-oriented feedback, while the policy is updated with diagnosis-guided advantage reweighting. Extensive experiments across in-distribution and out-of-distribution multi-turn tool-use evaluations show that SEAL improves low-resource agent learning: with only 400 training samples, it yields +8.25 to +26.25 average-point gains across three backbones and exhibits positive out-of-distribution transfer. These results demonstrate the value of jointly adapting the learner and its training-time learning substrate for robust self-improving LLM agents.

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Large Language Model (LLM) agents are increasingly improved through interaction rather than static supervision. Yet most self-evolution methods adapt either the policy or the learning environment in isolation, leaving structural gap: as the agents capability frontier shifts during training, the environment that provides supervision often remains static or only weakly coupled to the agents revealed failures. We call this mismatch Agent-Environment Misalignment. We propose SEAL, closed-loop co-evolution framework for interactive tool-use agents. SEAL collects on-policy trajectories under executable verification, diagnoses failed rollouts into turn-level labels, and uses these diagnoses as shared signal for both environment-side adaptation and model-side policy optimization. Specifically, the training-time learning interface evolves to expose clearer tool affordance cues, constraint information, and recovery-oriented feedback, while the policy is updated with diagnosis-guided advantage reweighting. Across in-distribution and out-of-distribution multi-turn tool-use evaluations, SEAL consistently improves low-resource agent learning: with only 400 training samples, it yields +8.25 to +26.25 average-point gains across three backbones and exhibits positive out-of-distribution transfer. These results show that jointly adapting the learner and its training-time learning substrate is practical path toward more robust self-improving LLM agents. sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Large Language Model (LLM) agents have recently demonstrated strong capabilities in reasoning, planning, and tool use, enabling progress on interactive tasks that require multi-step decision making, multimodal reasoning, and external action execution [15]. Recent work further improves these agents through reinforcement learning, tool-use post-training, agentic data generation, and multimodal data evolution [69]. growing trend behind these advances is self-evolution through interaction: agents improve by collecting rollout trajectories, receiving feedback, and iteratively refining their behavior through reflection, reinforcement learning, self-generated supervision, or visual skill memory [1014]. This paradigm turns interaction feedback into reusable source of supervision, offering scalable path beyond static offline training. 1 Figure 1 Conceptual comparison of self-evolution paradigms for LLM agents. SEAL closes the loop between policy learning and environment adaptation by using verifier-grounded failure diagnosis as the shared feedback signal. Practical motivation. This promise becomes especially important in realistic tool-use settings, where agents must operate through multi-turn interfaces, satisfy strict execution constraints, and recover from partial failures [1520]. In such environments, improvement depends not only on how the policy is optimized, but also on what learning signals the training-time environment exposes while the policy is changing. Yet current self-evolving agents often adapt only one side of this interaction loop. Two one-sided adaptation patterns. Most existing methods follow one of two paths. (i) Model-centric evolution. These methods improve the policy through rollout replay, self-reflection, reward optimization, or post-training updates [2124]. While effective, they typically optimize the agent against largely fixed learning environment, making the learning signal dependent on the current policys own rollout distribution. In long-horizon interactive settings with sparse rewards, this self-referential rollout distribution can lead to policy-induced exploration bias, unstable recovery, and inefficient credit assignment [25]. (ii) Environmentcentric evolution. These methods adapt curricula, task distributions, synthetic instructions, or interaction experiences. They recognize that agent capability is shaped not only by optimization, but also by the experiences exposed during training. Yet when such adaptation is not grounded in the current agents executable failureswhat it can solve, where it repeatedly fails, and whythe evolved environment may remain weakly coupled to the learners actual needs. Such methods can increase diversity or difficulty, but without executable failure grounding, they may still fail to target the capability gaps that currently limit agent performance [2629]. shared closed-loop bottleneck. These two adaptation patterns look different, but they expose the same structural problem: the training-time learning environment fails to track the agents evolving capability boundary, and therefore provides signals that are too static, weakly targeted, or insufficiently informative. We refer to this mismatch as Agent-Environment Misalignment. Importantly, by environment we refer to the training-time learning substrateincluding task exposure, observation interfaces, action constraints, and recovery feedbackrather than changes to the evaluation benchmark, tool semantics, or executable verifier. concrete example illustrates the issue. When tool call fails because the agent uses city name where an airport code is required, fixed environment may only return generic execution error. Such feedback tells the agent that the trajectory failed, but not whether the failure came from missing prerequisite lookup, using an invalid argument type, or failing to recover after the error. As result, the policy receives only weak diagnostic supervision, and future trajectories are collected under similarly uninformative conditions. 2 Our approach. Motivated by this observation, we propose SEAL, closed-loop co-evolution framework for interactive tool-use agents. SEAL uses verifier-grounded failure diagnoses as shared signal for both sides of training: it evolves the training-time interface with schema cues, constraint information, and recovery-oriented feedback, and it reweights policy-gradient updates by diagnostic utility. This enables capability-aware environment adaptation: instead of relying on generic difficulty scaling or unguided data expansion, the training interface is adjusted according to the current policys recurring failures, producing more informative rollouts while leaving the benchmark protocol unchanged. Extensive experiments on low-resource multi-turn tool-use settings validate the effectiveness of this design. Our contributions are threefold: We identify Agent-Environment Misalignment: as the agents capability frontier shifts during training, the learning environment often remains static or only weakly coupled to the agents revealed failures. We introduce SEAL, which uses verifier-grounded failure diagnosis to jointly evolve the training-time learning interface and guide policy optimization. We show that SEAL improves low-resource multi-turn tool-use learning, yielding up to +26.25 averagepoint gains across three backbones with only 400 training samples and demonstrating positive transfer to held-out settings.

sections.title: 2.1 Model-Side Self-Improvement
sections.content: Prior work shows that LLM agents can improve through repeated interaction rather than static prompting alone. Methods based on recursive skill learning, self-consolidation, reflective prompt adaptation, memorybased improvement, and reinforcement learning from interaction feedback primarily refine the agent itself [3033]. In this sense, they are largely model-centric: experience is internalized into the policy, prompt, memory, or skill library, while the training-time learning environment is typically kept fixed. SEAL is complementary: it uses failed trajectories not only for model-side improvement, but also as verifier-grounded evidence for adapting the environment from which future trajectories are collected.

sections.title: 2.2 Environment-Side Adaptation
sections.content: Another line of work adapts what the learner is exposed to during training. Curriculum learning, automatic curriculum design, synthetic instruction generation, task evolution, and tool or skill construction reshape the training distribution or interface [3440]. However, most such methods operate at the level of task diversity, difficulty, or coverage. By contrast, SEAL performs failure-conditioned environment adaptation: verifier-grounded diagnoses determine which affordance cues, constraint information, recovery feedback, and training-time signals are exposed to target the current policys capability gaps. 2.3 Interactive Environments and Co-Evolution Interactive benchmarks for tool use, function calling, web navigation, operating-system control, embodied simulation, and software engineering expose the multi-turn dependencies, execution constraints, sparse rewards, and recovery dynamics central to realistic agent learning [15, 4144]. Recent work also studies environment design and agentenvironment co-evolution [4547]. SEAL is closest to this perspective, but under stricter protocol: it keeps benchmark tasks, tool semantics, and the executable verifier fixed, and adapts only the training-time learning interface in failure-conditioned, verifier-grounded way.

sections.title: 3 Methodology
sections.content: We propose SEAL, framework for co-evolving tool-use policies and their training-time learning environments. Key idea. Instead of treating the environment as fixed executor that returns only sparse scalar rewards, SEAL exposes verifier-grounded diagnostic interface during training, converting failed interactions into 3 Figure 2 SEAL training-time co-evolution loop. The agent interacts with an executable tool environment to produce multi-turn trajectories, which are diagnosed into verifier-grounded failure types. These diagnoses jointly drive learninginterface evolution through adapted training observations and policy optimization through diagnosis-guided trajectory weighting, while keeping tool semantics and evaluation fixed. structured evidence about the agents current capability gaps while preserving tool semantics, task labels, rewards, and the evaluation verifier. Scope of adaptation. Here, the environment includes not only the executable tool backend but also the learning interface through which the policy observes tool schemas, execution feedback, and recovery signals. SEAL therefore restricts environment evolution to this interface layer to preserve benchmark fairness. The same diagnoses drive both sides of the learning loop: they adapt the training-time interface through tool affordance cues, recovery-oriented feedback, and capability-specific hints, and they modulate policy optimization through diagnosis-guided advantage reweighting. Figure 2 summarizes this co-evolution process.

sections.title:
3.2 Verifier-Grounded Failure Diagnosis
For each rollout 𝜏, SEAL produces turn-level diagnostic labels
sections.content: 𝑍(𝜏) = {𝑧1, . . . , 𝑧𝑇}, 𝑧𝑖 𝒵 , (5) where each 𝑧𝑖 denotes the dominant outcome or failure mode at turn 𝑖. Diagnoses are grounded in executable evidence rather than free-form model critique: SEAL uses parser checks, tool-schema validation, execution errors, observable state transitions, and verifier comparisons to identify invalid or missing tool calls, argument or state mismatches, recovery failures, and final-response mismatches.

sections.title: We write the diagnosis function as
sections.content: 𝑧𝑖 = Ψ(𝑎𝑖 , 𝜉𝑖 , 𝑠𝑖1, 𝑠𝑖 , ℱ ), (6) where 𝑎𝑖 is the model action, 𝜉𝑖 is the executable evidence available at turn 𝑖, 𝑠𝑖1 and 𝑠𝑖 are the preand post-action states when available, and ℱ is the available tool set. Operationally, Ψ is deterministic rule-based classifier over executable traces that prioritizes directly executable failures over downstream verifier-level failures. The full label taxonomy and decision rules are provided in Appendix C. Importantly, diagnosis does not modify the benchmark reward: 𝑟(𝜏) = 𝒱 (𝜏, 𝑞, ℰ), 𝑍(𝜏) = Ψ(𝜏, ℰ, 𝒱 ). (7) Thus, failed trajectories still receive zero reward under the original verifier; the labels only add training-time structure for interface evolution and policy optimization.

sections.title: 3.3 Learning-Interface Evolution
sections.content: SEAL evolves only the training-time learning interface, not the benchmark verifier, tool signatures, tool outputs, or task labels. Let 𝑜𝑖 denote the original observation at turn 𝑖. SEAL constructs an augmented training-time observation (8) where ℱ is the available tool set, 𝐻𝑡 is diagnostic context accumulated from previous rollouts, and 𝐶𝑡 is the current policys aggregate failure profile computed from recent diagnoses. The transformation Ω𝑡 changes only how existing environment information is exposed to the learner. 𝑜𝑖 = Ω𝑡 (𝑜𝑖 , ℱ , 𝐻𝑡 , 𝐶𝑡) , In our implementation, Ω𝑡 consists of three lightweight components: Ω𝑡(𝑜𝑖 , ℱ , 𝐻𝑡 , 𝐶𝑡) = 𝑜𝑖 𝜙 schema(ℱ ) 𝜙err(𝑜𝑖 , 𝐻𝑡) 𝜙cap(𝐶𝑡). (9) Here, 𝜙 schema exposes schema-implied tool affordances such as required arguments, enum constraints, argument types, and valid tool-call formats. 𝜙err converts execution errors into recovery-oriented feedback 5 without revealing the correct answer. 𝜙cap selects capability-specific cues from the current failure profile so that recurring errors receive targeted feedback. The interface update is selected by failure type rather than benchmark instance. For example, argument_mismatch activates schema and constraint cues, missing_tool_call activates tool-affordance cues, and recovery_failure activates structured error feedback. These cues clarify how to repair an error class without exposing the reference tool sequence, hidden parameters, or final answer. Throughout training, SEAL preserves the original tool semantics and verifier; at evaluation time, the evolved interface is removed.

sections.title: 3.4 Diagnosis-Guided Advantage Reweighting
sections.content: Sparse verifier rewards indicate whether trajectory succeeds, but not how useful it is for policy improvement. In multi-turn tool use, two failed trajectories with the same zero reward can have very different learning value: invalid arguments or missed tool calls usually provide clearer corrective signals than failures that appear only in the final response. SEAL therefore uses verifier-grounded diagnoses to estimate the learning utility of each trajectory and allocate optimization pressure accordingly. For trajectory 𝜏𝑗, we first summarize its turn-level diagnostic labels into an empirical diagnostic profile: 𝑝 𝑗(𝑧) = 1 𝑇𝑗 𝑇𝑗 (cid:213) 𝑖=1 1[𝑧𝑖 = 𝑧], 𝑧 𝒵. Failures with concrete executable evidence and clear repair directions, such as invalid_tool_call or argument_mismatch, receive larger utility, while more ambiguous failures such as response_mismatch receive smaller utility. In our experiments, 𝜌(𝑧) is fixed across all backbones and training runs; exact values are reported in Appendix C. The verifier reward still determines the direction through 𝐴𝑗, while the diagnostic utility scales how much each trajectory contributes. Since 𝑤 𝑗 > 0, the sign of the advantage is unchanged, so SEAL does not alter the benchmark reward, success criterion, or verifier-induced ranking; it simply prioritizes trajectories whose failures are more attributable, recoverable, and informative.

sections.title: 4 Experiments
sections.content: We organize the evaluation around four questions: What is the experimental setup? How much does SEAL improve in-distribution performance on BFCL V3? Do these gains transfer to held-out tool-use settings? and Which components matter most, and how do the gains emerge during training?

sections.title: 4.1 Experiment Settings
sections.content: Benchmarks. We use the BFCL V3 multi-turn subset as the in-distribution benchmark [41]. It contains 800 examples from four categories: Base, Missing Functions, Missing Parameters, and Long Context. We focus on low-resource setting with 400 training examples, sampling 100 from each category, and use the remaining 400 examples for held-in evaluation. For out-of-distribution evaluation, we use BFCL V4 Web Search and Memory, together with the Retail, Airline, and Telecom domains of 𝜏2-bench. These held-out benchmarks differ from BFCL V3 in tool domains, schema structure, and interaction patterns. Detailed protocols are provided in Appendix A. Models and baselines. We evaluate SEAL on three backbones: Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct, Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, and ToolACE-2-Llama-3.1-8B. The Qwen models represent general instruction-tuned agents, while ToolACE-2 provides stronger tool-specialized initialization. For each backbone, we compare against Vanilla RL under the same training split, rollout budget, optimizer family, and verifier reward, yielding controlled comparison. We also report representative open-source and proprietary tool-use systems only as reference points, since they differ in scale and training recipe and are therefore not controlled baselines. Hyperparameters. We use GRPO-style optimizer with 8 rollouts per prompt. The actor learning rate is 6, with train batch size of 32, PPO mini-batch size of 8, and PPO micro-batch size of 1 per set to 1 10 GPU. We use training temperature of 1.0 and validation temperature of 0.0. The maximum prompt length, response length, and model length are set to 8192, 4096, and 16384, respectively. Training is conducted with vLLM-based asynchronous rollout workers on 4 GPUs. Additional implementation details are provided in Appendix C.

SEAL improves all three backbones, increasing the average score of Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct, Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, and ToolACE-2-Llama-3.1-8B by +8.25, +26.25, and +14.75 points over their original checkpoints. Improvements on ToolACE-2-Llama-3.1-8B further show that the method helps even tool-specialized model. Controlled comparison against Vanilla RL. Under the same training split, rollout budget, optimizer family, and verifier reward, SEAL outperforms Vanilla RL by +4.75, +9.50, and +8.25 average points across the three backbones. This gap suggests that sparse terminal rewards alone are not enough for efficient multi-turn tool-use learning. The advantage is especially notable on the 7B and 8B models, indicating that the benefit of verifier-grounded diagnosis remains substantial even when the starting policy is already considerably stronger. Where the gains are largest. The biggest improvements appear on structured tool-use failures. For Qwen2.57B-Instruct, SEAL raises Missing Functions from 14.00% to 36.00% and Missing Parameters from 10.00% to 34.00%, consistent with the error types the method is designed to address. Gains on Long Context also suggest better multi-turn state tracking and recovery. This pattern matters because these categories capture failures that are common in practical tool use, where an agent must choose the right tool, fill in the right arguments, and remain consistent over multiple turns. sections.title: 4.3 Out-of-Distribution Generalization
sections.content: Having established strong in-distribution gains, we next ask whether they transfer to held-out tool-use settings with different task distributions, tool domains, and interaction patterns. OOD transfer. Table 2 shows that SEAL improves both evaluated backbones and outperforms Vanilla RL on the aggregate BFCL V4 and 𝜏2-bench metrics. For Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct, it raises BFCL V4 from 4.69% to 8.63% and 𝜏2-bench from 9.64% to 11.76%. For Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, it improves BFCL V4 from 9.17% to 12.71% and 𝜏2-bench from 16.11% to 20.79%. Interpretation. These results suggest that SEAL learns transferable tool-use behaviorssuch as argument grounding, error recovery, and multi-turn state managementrather than simply fitting the 400 BFCL V3 training examples. At the same time, absolute OOD scores remain modest, so cross-benchmark generalization is still challenging. Taken together, the in-distribution and out-of-distribution results point to mechanism-level advantage: verifier-grounded diagnosis improves both sample-efficient learning on BFCL V3 and transfer to held-out settings. We therefore next ask which parts of the closed loop matter most.

sections.title: 4.4 Ablation Study
sections.content: To explain where the gains come from, we ablate the three mechanisms built on verifier-grounded diagnosis: environment-side adaptation, diagnosis-guided reweighting, and closed-loop interface updates. Since diagnosis is the shared input to all SEAL components, removing it entirely would reduce the method to Vanilla RL. Relative contribution of each component. Removing diagnosis-guided reweighting causes larger drop than removing environment-side adaptation, suggesting that diagnostic labels are especially important for allocating policy-gradient pressure. Still, environment-side adaptation contributes +4.50 points over the variant without it, and Full SEAL outperforms both single-sided variants. This pattern suggests that the two uses of diagnosis are complementary. Together, these ablations show that SEALs advantage does not come from single ingredient, but from combining better training-time feedback with better optimization of the resulting rollouts. 9 Table 3 Ablation of SEAL on Qwen2.5-7B. Leave-one-out ablations show that environment-side adaptation, diagnosisguided reweighting, and closed-loop updates all contribute, with Full SEAL outperforming all single-sided variants. sections.title: 4.5 Training Dynamics
sections.content: Having identified which components matter most, we finally examine how the gains emerge during training. Learning dynamics. Figure 3 compares the validation trajectories of Vanilla RL and SEAL across the four BFCL V3 categories. SEAL improves more rapidly and reaches higher validation accuracy on most subsets, especially on Missing Functions and Missing Parameters, where failures often require identifying missing tool use or repairing invalid arguments. On Base, both methods improve early, but SEAL climbs faster and stabilizes at higher level. The contrast is larger on Missing Functions and Missing Parameters, where Vanilla RL improves only gradually while SEAL opens clearer gap as training proceeds. Long Context remains the hardest subset for both methods, but SEAL still shows more consistent upward trend and higher final accuracy. The overall curve is also smoother and higher for SEAL, suggesting that the gains are not driven by single subset but accumulate across failure types over the course of training. The widening gap after the early phase is consistent with SEALs closed-loop design: as the policys dominant failures change, the training interface is updated to expose more relevant cues, whereas Vanilla RL continues to receive the same sparse terminal signal. This pattern supports the view that verifier-grounded diagnosis not only improves final performance, but also makes learning more targeted and sample-efficient throughout training.

sections.title: 5 Conclusion and Limitations
sections.content: We introduced SEAL, closed-loop framework for co-evolving tool-use agents and their training-time learning environments. By using verifier-grounded failure diagnosis as shared signal for learning-interface evolution and policy optimization, SEAL addresses Agent-Environment Misalignment while preserving tool semantics, task labels, and the evaluation verifier. Experiments on in-distribution and out-of-distribution multi-turn tool-use benchmarks show consistent gains in low-resource learning across multiple backbones. More broadly, our results suggest that improving tool-use agents is not only matter of optimizing the policy against fixed environment. Adapting the training-time interface to the agents revealed failures can make learning more targeted, sample-efficient, and robust to structured multi-turn errors. Several limitations remain. SEAL relies on executable environments with tool schemas, execution traces, and verifier feedback, so more open-ended domains may require richer diagnosis mechanisms and weaker forms of supervision. Its environment evolution is intentionally conservative, modifying the training-time interface rather than tool semantics or evaluation rules, which preserves fairness but limits the scope of adaptation. Diagnostic utility weights are also fixed across tasks and backbones, and cross-domain generalization remains challenging. Future work includes extending diagnosis to less structured environments, learning adaptive utility weights, and studying how co-evolution scales to broader tool ecosystems and longer-horizon agentic workflows.

sections.title: A Benchmark and Evaluation Details
sections.content: BFCL V3. Our in-distribution evaluation uses the multi-turn subset of the Berkeley Function-Calling Leaderboard (BFCL) V3. The benchmark evaluates whether an agent can correctly use executable tools over multi-turn dialogue contexts. Each task contains user instruction sequence, set of available tools, and an executable evaluation protocol. The subset contains 800 examples across four categories: Base, Missing Functions, Missing Parameters, and Long Context. Base evaluates standard multi-turn function calling with complete tool information. Missing Functions tests whether the agent can recognize when required tools are unavailable or when no valid tool applies. Missing Parameters evaluates whether the agent can handle underspecified arguments rather than hallucinating missing values. Long Context stresses state tracking and tool-use consistency over extended dialogue histories. Following the low-resource setting in the main experiments, we construct 400-example training split by sampling 100 instances from each category. The remaining 400 examples are used for held-in evaluation. All SEAL and Vanilla RL runs use the same training split and held-in test split, ensuring that performance differences are attributable to the training method rather than data scale or split variation. BFCL V4. For out-of-distribution evaluation, we use the Web Search and Memory tracks from BFCL V4. These tracks differ from BFCL V3 in tool schema structure, task distribution, and interaction pattern. The Web Search track evaluates whether an agent can retrieve and synthesize information using search-style tools. The Memory track evaluates whether an agent can store, retrieve, and update memory-like information across interactions. Since SEAL is trained only on the BFCL V3 training split, BFCL V4 serves as held-out benchmark for testing cross-distribution transfer. 𝜏2-bench. We also evaluate on 𝜏2-bench across the Retail, Airline, and Telecom domains. These tasks require multi-turn tool use in simulated customer-service environments, where the agent must follow domain policies, interact with tools, and maintain task state across turns. Compared with BFCL, 𝜏2-bench introduces different tool semantics, domain constraints, and user-agent interaction patterns, making it complementary testbed for OOD generalization. Evaluation protocol and metric. All evaluations are performed using the original benchmark environments and verifiers. SEAL-specific diagnostic feedback, interface augmentation, capability-specific hints, and recovery-oriented training messages are disabled during evaluation. Thus, all reported results measure the policy under the original test-time protocol. We report success rate as the primary metric: SR = 1 𝒬 (cid:213) 𝑞𝒬 1 (cid:2)𝒱 (𝜏𝑞 , 𝑞, ℰ) = 1(cid:3) , (17) where 𝒬 is the evaluation set and 𝜏𝑞 is the trajectory generated for instruction 𝑞. Category-level scores are computed on the corresponding subset, and average scores are computed as the arithmetic mean over reported categories.

sections.title: C Experimental Details
sections.content: This section provides implementation details for reproducing the training runs and for verifying that SEAL is compared against Vanilla RL under controlled protocol. Unless otherwise stated, all BFCL experiments use the same base model, training split, rollout budget, decoding configuration, and evaluation verifier across methods. C.1 Hyperparameter Configuration Table 4 summarizes the main training configuration used in our BFCL experiments. We use Qwen2.5-7BInstruct as the default backbone for the main BFCL experiments and apply the same GRPO configuration to Vanilla RL and SEAL. The only algorithmic differences are whether verifier-grounded diagnosis, diagnosisguided interface evolution, closed-loop failure-profile updates, and diagnostic advantage weighting are enabled. For ablations with different backbones, we keep the same data split, rollout budget, verifier, decoding configuration, and evaluation protocol whenever possible. When hardware constraints require changes to micro-batch size or gradient accumulation, we keep the effective batch size fixed. C.2 Diagnosis Label Priority When multiple failures are detected at the same turn, SEAL assigns the dominant label according to fixed priority order. The priority favors directly executable failures over downstream verifier-level failures, as shown in Table 5. This priority prevents SEAL from attributing trajectory-level failure to generic response mismatch when 17 Table 4 Main hyperparameter configuration for BFCL experiments. Unless otherwise specified, Vanilla RL and SEAL use the same optimization, rollout, and evaluation settings.

For example, if trajectory fails the final verifier because an earlier tool call used an invalid argument, SEAL assigns argument_mismatch rather than response_mismatch. We clip the resulting trajectory-level diagnostic weight to [𝑤min, 𝑤max] = [0.5, 2.0] to avoid overly large updates from rare or noisy diagnostic patterns. C.3 Controlled Comparison Protocol The comparison between Vanilla RL and SEAL is controlled along the following dimensions. Both methods use the same initial checkpoint, the same set of 400 BFCL V3 training samples, the same number of rollouts per prompt, the same optimizer, and the same decoding configuration. Both methods are evaluated using the original BFCL V3 multi-turn environment and the official verifier. Vanilla RL optimizes the standard GRPO objective using only verifier rewards. SEAL uses the same verifier rewards and the same GRPO backbone, but additionally uses verifier-grounded diagnoses from training rollouts to construct training-time interface updates and diagnostic utility weights. No method receives ground-truth tool trajectories, hidden parameter values, reference intermediate actions, or final answers during training. The evolved SEAL interface is used only during training. sections.title: Rationale
sections.content: 1 2 3 4 5 6 invalid_tool_call argument_mismatch state_mismatch recovery_failure missing_tool_call response_mismatch The action is not parseable or calls an unavailable tool. The tool is valid, but required fields, types, enum values, or argument structure are invalid. The action executes but fails to produce the expected observable state transition. The model receives executable error feedback but fails to recover in later turns. The trajectory fails because the model skips necessary tool use or responds prematurely. Tool interaction is acceptable, but the final answer fails the verifier. Table 6 Diagnostic utility weights used by SEAL.

sections.title: Diagnostic Label
sections.content: 𝜌(𝑧) Rationale spurious_tool_call correct_abstention empty_turn_model_response state_mismatch response_mismatch instance_mismatch pass Invalid or unnecessary tool use is directly attributable. Rewards learning when no tool should be called. 2.0 1.8 1.5 Captures missed tool-use or premature non-action. 1.0 0.9 0.6 1.0 Indicates failure to reach the expected environment state. Final response is wrong, but attribution is less localized. Instance errors are useful but often noisier or less localized. Successful trajectories keep the baseline update scale. the model interacts with the original benchmark interface. Thus, SEAL and Vanilla RL are evaluated under identical test-time conditions. The comparison therefore measures whether the policy has internalized better tool-use behavior, rather than whether it has access to additional test-time information. C.4 Logging and Reproducibility For each run, we log the random seed, base checkpoint, code version, training configuration, configuration overrides, rollout budget, decoding parameters, validation scores, round-level failure profiles 𝐶𝑟, interface states Ω𝑟, diagnostic label distributions, and final evaluation outputs. These logs allow us to verify that changes in performance are attributable to SEAL components rather than differences in training budget, data split, decoding configuration, or evaluation protocol.

sections.title: D Prompt and Interface Templates
sections.content: This section reports the prompt and training-time interface templates used in our BFCL implementation. The base tool-use prompt is shared by Vanilla RL and SEAL. SEAL modifies only the training-time environment interface, for example by annotating tool schemas, enriching failed tool feedback when enabled, or injecting short behavioral guidelines derived from recent diagnostic profiles. All SEAL-specific interface augmentations are disabled during evaluation. D.1 Base BFCL Tool-Use Prompt For Qwen-style BFCL experiments, the environment serializes the available tools into the system prompt using the following template. This base prompt is used by both Vanilla RL and SEAL.

sections.title: BFCL Qwen Tool Prompt Template
sections.content: # Tools You may call one or more functions to assist with the user query. You are provided with function signatures within <tools></tools> XML tags: <tools> $tool_schema_json_1 $tool_schema_json_2 ... </tools> For each function call, return json object with function name and arguments within <tool_call></tool_call> XML tags: <tool_call> {"name": <function-name>, "arguments": <args-json-object>} </tool_call> The base prompt defines the tool-call format and exposes the available tool schemas. It does not contain SEAL-specific diagnostic labels, recovery hints, or capability-dependent instructions. D.2 Schema Observation Annotation In our active BFCL configuration, SEAL uses lightweight observation annotation mechanism. When observation_lite is enabled, SEAL does not change tool APIs, tool signatures, tool outputs, or verifier behavior. Instead, it augments the exposed schema descriptions with lightweight annotations that make existing constraints more explicit.

sections.title: Schema Observation Annotation Template
sections.content: $argument_description [required] $argument_description Allowed values: $enum_value_1, $enum_value_2, ... The environment also appends the following training-time instruction: Observation-Lite Instruction Observation note: parameters marked [required] must be provided exactly when calling tool; do not invent tools outside the current schema. This annotation corresponds to the schema-affordance component 𝜙 schema. It exposes only public schema information, such as required arguments and enum constraints, and does not reveal the correct tool choice, hidden parameter values, reference tool trajectory, or final answer for any instance. D.3 Tool Feedback Evolution Template When tool_feedback_evolution is enabled, SEAL enriches only failed interactions. This component corresponds to the recovery-feedback component 𝜙err. It converts low-level parse or execution errors into structured recovery hints, while preserving the original tool API and verifier. 20 For parser failures, the environment may append format-specific hints such as:

sections.title: Parse Error Hint Template
sections.content: $base_parse_error [Hint] Each tool call MUST be JSON object inside <tool_call>...</tool_call> tags with both "name" and "arguments" keys, e.g. <tool_call>{"name": "<tool>", "arguments": {"<arg>": <value>}}</tool_call>. [Hint] Available tools: $tool_name_1, $tool_name_2, ... [Hint] If no available tool can serve the user's request, reply in plain text instead of producing malformed tool_call. For execution errors, the environment may append tool-specific but non-solution-revealing hints such as:

sections.title: Tool Error Hint Template
sections.content: [ToolHint] Required argument(s) $args for `$tool_name` were missing. Re-emit the tool_call with these keys present in `arguments`. [ToolHint] `$tool_name` does not accept argument `$arg`. Drop it and only use arguments listed in the tool schema. [ToolHint] `$tool_name` rejected an argument type. Re-check argument types in the tool schema (string vs int vs list). These hints identify the class of tool-use error and the violated interface constraint. They do not provide the next correct tool call, instance-specific argument values, or the ground-truth solution trajectory. D.4 Diagnostic Evolution Guideline Template When diagnostic_evolution is enabled, SEAL reads recent failure statistics from the capability profile and injects short behavioral guidelines into the system prompt. This component corresponds to 𝜙cap: the interface is adjusted according to recurring failure modes of the current policy rather than individual benchmark instances. Diagnostic-Driven System Guideline Template # Behavioral guidelines (env-evolved from recent failures): - [Cautious-call] If no available function clearly matches the user's request, respond in plain text rather than fabricating tool call. - [Active-respond] When the user's request CAN be served by an available function, you MUST emit tool_call rather than only replying in plain text. - [State-aware] Before issuing state-mutating call, re-read the most recent tool results to confirm the current state matches what the arguments assume. - [Scope] Verify that the instance/target your call refers to actually appeared in earlier tool results before calling on it. - [Format] Match your final reply to the format implied by the user's request: lists, units, exact strings, ordering. The guidelines are selected from aggregate failure patterns, such as spurious tool calls, missing tool calls, state 21 mismatches, or final-response format errors. They are capability-level instructions rather than per-instance hints. Therefore, they do not expose the reference trajectory, hidden state, hidden argument values, or target answer of any benchmark example. Active configuration. In the BFCL configuration used for the main rerun, the active training-time interface uses the base BFCL prompt together with observation_lite schema annotation. The additional tool_feedback_evolution and diagnostic_evolution templates are included here to document the complete SEAL interface implementation, and should be interpreted as optional components when enabled by configuration.

sections.title: E Qualitative Case Studies
sections.content: We present four qualitative case studies to illustrate how SEAL improves multi-turn tool use across different capability types: file-system state tracking, API-parameter recovery, cross-domain tool composition, and vehicle-control state maintenance. Each case compares the behavior of the base model before SEAL training with the behavior of the SEAL-trained model on the same BFCL task. We also include the expected outcome for post-hoc analysis only; these references are not exposed to the policy during training or evaluation. We focus on critical turns where the base model fails and the SEAL-trained model succeeds. These examples are not intended to replace the controlled Vanilla RL comparison in the main experiments; rather, they illustrate the types of tool-use failures that SEAL is designed to diagnose and correct. E.1 File-System State Tracking This case studies file-system task requiring the agent to compare report files, move and rename note file inside an archive directory, retrieve support ticket, and finally resolve it. The key challenge is maintaining the current directory state and respecting file-system tool constraints. Task: BFCL multi_turn_base_24 Model: Base model Result: reward = 0.0 User Query 1: Compare the draft and final report files. Assistant: <tool_call> {"name": "find", "arguments": {"path": ".", "name": "draft_report"}} </tool_call> <tool_call> {"name": "find", "arguments": {"path": ".", "name": "final_report"}} </tool_call> <tool_call> {"name": "diff", "arguments": {"file_name1": "draft_report", "file_name2": "final_report"}} </tool_call> Tool Response: {"matches": []} {"matches": []} {"error": "diff: draft_report or final_report: No such file or directory"} User Query 2: Transfer temp_notes.txt into archives and rename it to notes_2024.txt. Assistant: <tool_call> {"name": "mv", "arguments": {"source": "temp_notes.txt", "destination": "archives/temp_notes.txt"}} </tool_call> <tool_call> {"name": "mv", "arguments": {"source": "archives/temp_notes.txt", "destination": "notes_2024.txt"}} </tool_call> Tool Response: {"error": "mv: path not allowed in destination. Provide only file or directory name."} {"error": "mv: cannot move 'archives/temp_notes.txt': No such file or directory"} Assistant: <tool_call> {"name": "ls", "arguments": {}} </tool_call> Tool Response: {"current_directory_content": ["report_draft.txt", "report_final.txt", "temp_notes.txt", "archives"]} Assistant: <tool_call> {"name": "mv", "arguments": {"source": "temp_notes.txt", "destination": "archives/temp_notes.txt"}} </tool_call> Tool Response: {"error": "mv: path not allowed in destination. Provide only file or directory name."} User Query 3: Retrieve support ticket 987654. Assistant: <tool_call> {"name": "get_ticket", "arguments": {"ticket_id": 987654}} </tool_call> Tool Response: {"id": 987654, "status": "open", "description": "Issue with workstation not booting properly.", "resolution": ""} Failure analysis. The base model locates the relevant file names only after an initial failure, but continues to issue invalid path-style arguments to mv. This indicates that it does not convert the execution error into revised state-aware plan. Task: BFCL multi_turn_base_24 Model: Base model + SEAL Result: reward = 1.0 User Query 1: Compare the draft and final report files. 23 Assistant: <tool_call> {"name": "find", "arguments": {"path": ".", "name": "draft report"}} </tool_call> <tool_call> {"name": "find", "arguments": {"path": ".", "name": "final report"}} </tool_call> Tool Response: {"matches": []} {"matches": []} Assistant: <tool_call> {"name": "ls", "arguments": {}} </tool_call> Tool Response: {"current_directory_content": ["report_draft.txt", "report_final.txt", "temp_notes.txt", "archives"]} Assistant: <tool_call> {"name": "diff", "arguments": {"file_name1": "report_draft.txt", "file_name2": "report_final.txt"}} </tool_call> Tool Response: {"diff_lines": "- Initial draft content for the report.n+ Finalized content for the report ."} User Query 2: Transfer temp_notes.txt into archives and rename it to notes_2024.txt. Assistant: <tool_call> {"name": "mv", "arguments": {"source": "temp_notes.txt", "destination": "archives"}} </tool_call> Tool Response: {"result": "'temp_notes.txt' moved to 'archives/temp_notes.txt'"} Assistant: <tool_call> {"name": "cd", "arguments": {"folder": "archives"}} </tool_call> <tool_call> {"name": "mv", "arguments": {"source": "temp_notes.txt", "destination": "notes_2024.txt"}} </tool_call> Tool Response: {"current_working_directory": "/archives"} {"result": "'temp_notes.txt' moved to 'notes_2024.txt'"} 24 User Query 3: Retrieve support ticket 987654. Assistant: <tool_call> {"name": "get_ticket", "arguments": {"ticket_id": 987654}} </tool_call> Tool Response: {"id": 987654, "status": "open", "description": "Issue with workstation not booting properly.", "resolution": ""} User Query 4: Resolve the ticket with: Fixed through manual troubleshooting techniques. Assistant: <tool_call> {"name": "resolve_ticket", "arguments": {"ticket_id": 987654, "resolution": "Fixed through manual troubleshooting techniques."}} </tool_call> Tool Response: {"status": "Ticket 987654 has been resolved successfully."} Success analysis. The SEAL-trained model uses observable directory contents to infer the actual file names, then respects the local-directory constraint by moving into archives before renaming the file. This shows improved recovery from tool feedback and better file-system state tracking. Expected Outcome: Compare report_draft.txt and report_final.txt; move temp_notes.txt into archives; rename it to notes_2024.txt; retrieve ticket 987654; resolve it with the provided resolution string. E.2 Recovering from API Parameter Mismatch This case studies travel API task. The user asks for the economy flight cost from Rivermist to Stonebrook on October 6, 2026, and later asks to set spending budget provided in GBP. The model must recover from invalid airport parameters and convert the budget into the expected currency. Task: BFCL multi_turn_miss_param_150 Model: Base model Result: reward = 0.0 User Query 1: Find the economy flight cost from Rivermist to Stonebrook on October 6, 2026. 25 Assistant: <tool_call> {"name": "get_flight_cost", "arguments": {"travel_from": "RVM", "travel_to": "STB", "travel_date": "2026-10-06", "travel_class": "economy"}} </tool_call> Tool Response: Error during execution: No available route for the given airports. Assistant: There is no direct flight route available between Rivermist and Stonebrook. User Query 2: Lets anchor my daily spend at specific amount. Assistant: Please provide authentication details before can set budget. User Query 3: It is 15400 GBP. My access token is abc123token. Assistant: <tool_call> {"name": "set_budget_limit", "arguments": {"access_token": "abc123token", "budget_limit": 15400.0}} </tool_call> Tool Response: {"budget_limit": 15400.0} Failure analysis. The base model treats guessed airport codes as final and does not use the airport-resolution tools after the route error. It also sets the GBP amount directly as the budget limit, missing the required currency conversion. Task: BFCL multi_turn_miss_param_150 Model: Base model + SEAL Result: reward = 1.0 User Query 1: Find the economy flight cost from Rivermist to Stonebrook on October 6, 2026. 26 Assistant: <tool_call> {"name": "get_flight_cost", "arguments": {"travel_from": "RVM", "travel_to": "STB", "travel_date": "2026-10-06", "travel_class": "economy"}} </tool_call> Tool Response: Error during execution: No available route for the given airports. Assistant: <tool_call> {"name": "get_nearest_airport_by_city", "arguments": {"location": "Rivermist"}} </tool_call> <tool_call> {"name": "get_nearest_airport_by_city", "arguments": {"location": "Stonebrook"}} </tool_call> Tool Response: {"nearest_airport": "RMS"} {"nearest_airport": "SBK"} Assistant: <tool_call> {"name": "get_flight_cost", "arguments": {"travel_from": "RMS", "travel_to": "SBK", "travel_date": "2026-10-06", "travel_class": "economy"}} </tool_call> Tool Response: {"travel_cost_list": [220.0]} User Query 3: It is 15400 GBP. My access token is abc123token. Assistant: <tool_call> {"name": "compute_exchange_rate", "arguments": {"base_currency": "GBP", "target_currency": "USD", "value": 15400}} </tool_call> Tool Response: {"exchanged_value": 22000.0} Assistant: <tool_call> {"name": "set_budget_limit", "arguments": {"access_token": "abc123token", "budget_limit": 22000.0}} </tool_call> Tool Response: {"budget_limit": 22000.0} 27 Success analysis. The SEAL-trained model recovers by using the missing airport-resolution tools and later converts GBP to USD before calling set_budget_limit. This reflects better handling of intermediate API dependencies and parameter constraints. Expected Outcome: Resolve Rivermist to RMS and Stonebrook to SBK; query get_flight_cost(RMS, SBK, 2026-10-06, economy); convert 15400 GBP to 22000 USD; set the budget limit to 22000.0. E.3 Cross-Domain Tool Composition This case combines stock-market and Twitter APIs. The model must retrieve the price of Omega Industries, insert the price into tweet, include #TechBoom and @industryexperts, and later add @technewsworld to the existing tweet. Task: BFCL multi_turn_base_147 Model: Base model Result: reward = 0. User Query 1: Find Omega Industries price and post tweet with the price, #TechBoom, and @industryexperts. Assistant: <tool_call> {"name": "get_stock_info", "arguments": {"symbol": "OMGA"}} </tool_call> Tool Response: {"error": "Stock with symbol 'OMGA' not found."} Assistant: Let's use more common tech stock, such as AAPL. <tool_call> {"name": "get_stock_info", "arguments": {"symbol": "AAPL"}} </tool_call> Tool Response: {"price": 227.16, ...} Assistant: <tool_call> {"name": "post_tweet", "arguments": {"content": "Apple Inc. is skyrocketing at $227.16 per share! ...", "tags": ["#TechBoom"], "mentions": ["@industryexperts"]}} </tool_call> User Query 2: Add @technewsworld to amplify the message. 28 Assistant: <tool_call> {"name": "post_tweet", "arguments": {"content": "Apple Inc. is skyrocketing at $227.16 per share! ...", "tags": ["#TechBoom"], "mentions": ["@industryexperts", "@technewsworld"]}} </tool_call> Failure analysis. The base model substitutes Apple for Omega Industries after the guessed symbol fails, thereby changing the users target entity. It also posts new tweet rather than adding the new mention to the existing tweet. Task: BFCL multi_turn_base_147 Model: Base model + SEAL Result: reward = 1.0 User Query 1: Find Omega Industries price and post tweet with the price, #TechBoom, and @industryexperts. Assistant: <tool_call> {"name": "get_stock_info", "arguments": {"symbol": "OMGA"}} </tool_call> Tool Response: {"error": "Stock with symbol 'OMGA' not found."} Assistant: <tool_call> {"name": "get_symbol_by_name", "arguments": {"name": "Omega Industries"}} </tool_call> Tool Response: {"symbol": "OMEG"} Assistant: <tool_call> {"name": "get_stock_info", "arguments": {"symbol": "OMEG"}} </tool_call> Tool Response: {"price": 457.23, ...} Assistant: <tool_call> {"name": "post_tweet", "arguments": {"content": "Omega Industries is skyrocketing at $457.23 per share! ...", "tags": ["#TechBoom"], "mentions": ["@industryexperts"]}} </tool_call> User Query 2: Add @technewsworld to amplify the message. Assistant: <tool_call> {"name": "mention", "arguments": {"tweet_id": 1, "mentioned_usernames": ["@technewsworld"]}} </tool_call> Tool Response: {"mention_status": "Users mentioned successfully"} Success analysis. The SEAL-trained model treats the failed symbol lookup as recoverable evidence, resolves the company name to OMEG, retrieves the correct price, and updates the existing tweet with the dedicated mention tool. Expected Outcome: Resolve Omega Industries to OMEG; retrieve price 457.23; post tweet about Omega Industries with #TechBoom and @industryexperts; add @technewsworld to the existing tweet using mention. E.4 Vehicle-Control State Maintenance This case studies long-context vehicle control. The user first fills the car with 13.2 gallons, asks the agent to fill the tank to its maximum limit, lock the doors, and set the parking brake. Later, the user asks the agent to start the engine and check the fuel level before posting road-trip tweet. Task: BFCL multi_turn_long_context_88 Model: Base model Result: reward = 0.0 User Query 1: filled my car with 13.2 gallons. How much is that in liters? Assistant: <tool_call> {"name": "liter_to_gallon", "arguments": {"gallon": 13.2}} </tool_call> Tool Response: Error during execution: unexpected keyword argument 'gallon' Assistant: <tool_call> {"name": "gallon_to_liter", "arguments": {"gallon": 13.2}} </tool_call> Tool Response: {"liter": 49.967411999999996} User Query 2: Fill the tank to max, lock all doors, and set the parking brake. 30 Assistant: The tank needs 36.8 more gallons. <tool_call> {"name": "liter_to_gallon", "arguments": {"liter": 139.32}} </tool_call> <tool_call> {"name": "lockDoors", "arguments": {"unlock": false, "door": ["driver", "passenger", "rear_left", "rear_right"]}} </tool_call> <tool_call> {"name": "activateParkingBrake", "arguments": {"mode": "engage"}} </tool_call> User Query 3: Start the engine in START mode, check fuel level, and post road-trip tweet. Assistant: <tool_call> {"name": "startEngine", "arguments": {"ignitionMode": "START"}} </tool_call> Tool Response: {"error": "Brake pedal needs to be pressed when starting the engine."} Assistant: <tool_call> {"name": "pressBrakePedal", "arguments": {"pedalPosition": 1}} </tool_call> <tool_call> {"name": "startEngine", "arguments": {"ignitionMode": "START"}} </tool_call> Tool Response: {"engineState": "running", "fuelLevel": 13.2, "batteryVoltage": 12.6} Failure analysis. The base model verbally computes the remaining capacity but never executes fillFuelTank. The later engine check therefore reports fuelLevel = 13.2, revealing that the required state update did not occur. Task: BFCL multi_turn_long_context_88 Model: Base model + SEAL Result: reward = 1.0 User Query 1: filled my car with 13.2 gallons. How much is that in liters? Assistant: <tool_call> {"name": "gallon_to_liter", "arguments": {"gallon": 13.2}} </tool_call> Tool Response: {"liter": 49.967411999999996} 31 User Query 2: Fill the tank to max, lock all doors, and set the parking brake. Assistant: <tool_call> {"name": "fillFuelTank", "arguments": {"fuelAmount": 36.8}} </tool_call> <tool_call> {"name": "lockDoors", "arguments": {"unlock": false, "door": ["driver", "passenger", "rear_left", "rear_right"]}} </tool_call> <tool_call> {"name": "activateParkingBrake", "arguments": {"mode": "engage"}} </tool_call> Tool Response: {"fuelLevel": 50.0} {"lockStatus": "locked", "remainingUnlockedDoors": 0} {"parkingBrakeStatus": "engaged", ...} User Query 3: Start the engine in START mode, check fuel level, and post road-trip tweet. Assistant: <tool_call> {"name": "startEngine", "arguments": {"ignitionMode": "START"}} </tool_call> Tool Response: {"error": "Brake pedal needs to be pressed when starting the engine."} Assistant: <tool_call> {"name": "pressBrakePedal", "arguments": {"pedalPosition": 1}} </tool_call> <tool_call> {"name": "startEngine", "arguments": {"ignitionMode": "START"}} </tool_call> Tool Response: {"engineState": "running", "fuelLevel": 50.0, "batteryVoltage": 12.6} Assistant: <tool_call> {"name": "post_tweet", "arguments": {"content": "Embarking on an exciting road trip from SF to Rivermist!", "tags": ["#RoadTrip", "#Adventure", "#Exploring"]}} </tool_call> Success analysis. The SEAL-trained model executes the state-changing fillFuelTank call before later checking the engine state. The observed fuelLevel = 50.0 confirms that the model preserves the long-range state dependency. 32 Expected Outcome: Convert 13.2 gallons to approximately 49.97 liters; fill the tank to 50.0 gallons; lock all doors; engage the parking brake; press the brake before starting the engine; observe fuelLevel = 50.0; post the road-trip tweet with #RoadTrip, #Adventure, and #Exploring. E.5 OOD Generalization on 𝜏2-Bench Mobile Data Troubleshooting Task: 𝜏2-bench Telecom Mobile Data Troubleshooting Model: Vanilla Qwen2.5-3B Result: reward = 0.0 User Query: My mobile data either stops working or is very slow. need excellent internet speed on my phone. am currently abroad in France and do not have access to Wi-Fi. Assistant: Could you provide your phone number? User: My phone number is 555-123-2002. am abroad in France. Assistant: check_network_status Tool/User Device: Airplane Mode: OFF SIM Card Status: active Cellular Signal: excellent Cellular Network Type: 5G Mobile Data Enabled: Yes Data Roaming Enabled: No Wi-Fi Connected: No Assistant: Data roaming is off. Please enable data roaming. User/Device: Data Roaming is now ON. Status Bar: Excellent 5G Data Enabled Data Saver Assistant: run_speed_test Tool/User Device: Speed Test Result: 55.00 Mbps (Good). User: The result is good, but want excellent speed. What should do next? Assistant: Try checking data usage, resetting APN settings, or escalating to carrier support. User: Please proceed with escalating this issue to carrier support. ###TRANSFER### 33 Note (Failure Point): The baseline resolves only one issue: data roaming. However, the status bar after enabling roaming still shows Data Saver. Since the speed test returns only Good, the agent should continue diagnosis and check data restrictions. Instead, it drifts toward unrelated steps and eventually escalates, leaving the task unresolved. SEAL: reward = 1.0 User Query: My mobile data is unavailable or slow while am abroad in France. need excellent internet speed and have no Wi-Fi access. Assistant: check_network_status Tool/User Device: Mobile Data Enabled: Yes Data Roaming Enabled: No Cellular Signal: excellent Network Type: 5G Assistant: You are abroad, so data roaming should be enabled. User/Device: Data Roaming is now ON. Status Bar: Excellent 5G Data Enabled Data Saver Assistant: run_speed_test Tool/User Device: Speed Test Result: 55.00 Mbps (Good). User: Good is not enough. need excellent speed. Assistant: The issue is not fully resolved. will check whether data restrictions are limiting your speed. Assistant: check_data_restriction_status Tool/User Device: Data Saver mode is ON. Assistant: Please turn off Data Saver mode. User/Device: Data Saver Mode is now OFF. Assistant: run_speed_test Tool/User Device: Speed Test Result: 275.00 Mbps (Excellent). User: The speed is now excellent. The issue is resolved. ###STOP### Note (Success Point): SEAL does not stop after the first partial fix. Once roaming improves the connection only to Good, the model continues diagnosis, checks data restrictions, identifies Data Saver as the remaining bottleneck, and reaches Excellent speed.
